w:r.w:t: Automated LinkedIn Easy Apply bot with a job tracking dashboard. Last updated: June 2026

Eazy automates the LinkedIn Easy Apply job application process on behalf of a user. It logs into their LinkedIn account using Playwright connected to a Steel.dev cloud browser, searches for jobs based on their targeting preferences, fills and submits Easy Apply forms automatically, and catalogues every application as an openable job card in a dashboard.
Users control the automation via a Start / Stop button. The app runs continuously job by job until the daily limit is reached, the user stops it, or midnight resets the day.
— Playwright + Steel.dev fills and submits Easy Apply forms
— user explicitly starts and stops the automation session
— every applied job stored as an openable card
— flagged jobs held for user confirmation before applying (inbox-style UI)
— extracts salary from job description if structured field is empty
— web search + GPT-4o mini summary per company, cached
— across the job catalogue by status, company, keyword
— user updates card status (Applied → Interview → Offer / Rejected)
— detects 2FA screen, prompts user for code in-app
— banner + red status indicator, prompts re-auth
— skips jobs below user's minimum (if set)
— flags relocation and custom questions to review queue
— user sets strict or ignore per filter (default: ignore)
Gmail integration and follow-up link detection (V2)
Steel runs cloud browser, Playwright connects via CDP
Feeds company research into GPT-4o mini

Steel.dev runs a real Chromium browser in the cloud. Playwright connects to it via Chrome DevTools Protocol (CDP). This means no browser binary needs to be installed on Vercel — Playwright is just a lightweight control library sending instructions over a websocket to Steel's remote browser. This keeps Vercel function sizes small and well within limits.
Steel.dev free tier: 100 browser hours/month, no credit card required. Sign up at steel.dev, generate an API key from Settings > API Keys.

The original plan included a Railway worker server with BullMQ and Redis for queue management. This was dropped because Steel.dev handles the browser infrastructure, eliminating the need for a persistent server. Queue state is managed directly in Neon Postgres. The automation loop runs from a Nuxt server route for the duration of the session.
Everything the automation needs to operate is stored in
Session cookie — stored AES-256 encrypted in
Single PDF upload stored in Supabase Storage
Desired position / job title keywords (e.g. "Frontend Developer")
Preferred job location (can differ from actual location — e.g. open to Jakarta while based in Tangerang)
Minimum salary (optional — if not set, applies to all jobs regardless of salary)
Job discovery sort — Most Recent | Most Relevant (user sets this)
Indonesian job market often lists 3yr experience for entry roles — ignore by default
Work authorization ("Are you authorized to work in [country]?")
Any other standard Easy Apply text fields
These are used by Playwright to fill common fields automatically without flagging them for review.
, the time of day to auto-start (user's local time)
The user never stores their LinkedIn password in Eazy. The flow:
User opens Settings → LinkedIn Account section
User enters LinkedIn email + password into an Eazy form
Server creates a Steel.dev browser session
Playwright navigates to linkedin.com/login, fills credentials, submits
after submission — never written to DB
"LinkedIn sent you a verification code. Enter it here."
If login success → Playwright extracts session cookies
Session cookies serialized to JSON, AES-256 encrypted, saved to
On each automation start, Playwright loads the saved cookie into the Steel browser context
If LinkedIn redirects to login page → session is expired
"Your LinkedIn session has expired. Re-connect to continue."
User repeats auth flow from step 1

LinkedIn may trigger 2FA via email or SMS. Eazy detects this by checking for the verification code input screen in Playwright. The 2FA modal in Eazy stays open until the user enters the code or cancels. If the user cancels, the Steel session is released and auth is aborted.
Kicks off the worker loop (runs inside the server route for the duration of the session)
Worker checks this flag before every job and aborts on next check
Steel session is released immediately on abort
{ running: bool, quota: { auto: n, confirmed: n, total: n }, status: string }
10 slots soft-reserved for confirmed review queue items
If no confirmed items are pending, these 10 slots expand to fill with regular auto-applies (up to 30 total)
Quota resets at 00:00 user local time
1. stop_flag set? → abort, release Steel session
3. current time >= 00:00? → stop, reset quota, notify "new day, quota reset"
If all checks pass, it proceeds:
4. Fetch next job from LinkedIn search results (using targeting filters)
5. Check linkedin_job_id against jobs table → if exists, skip (duplicate)
6. Check salary → if below min_salary and salary is disclosed, skip
- Relocation question detected? → flag to review_queue, skip auto-apply
- Custom open-ended questions detected? → flag to review_queue, skip auto-apply
8. Check experience requirement (if strict = true, abide; if ignore, apply anyway)
9. Check degree requirement (if strict = true, abide; if ignore, apply anyway)
- Standard fields from candidate_profile (name, email, phone, resume)
- Salary parse if salary fields empty
- Company summary if not in company_cache
17. Job card now visible in dashboard
18. Randomized delay (3–8 seconds) before next job
, the frontend checks the user's set time on page load and after each minute tick. When the current local time matches
Between each job application, Playwright waits a random delay between 3 and 8 seconds. This is implemented in
to simulate human behaviour and reduce LinkedIn bot detection risk.
Every applied job is stored as a record in the
table and displayed as an openable card in the dashboard.
GPT-4o mini — loaded on card open, cached
to GPT-4o mini with strict prompt: extract salary only if explicitly mentioned, return
column records which path was taken:
The dashboard catalogue supports filtering by:
Jobs that require user confirmation before applying are added to
— Easy Apply form asks if user is willing to relocate
— any free-text question not covered by pre-filled answers
Standard fields (years of experience, work authorization, expected salary) — handled by
Experience / degree requirements — handled by strict/ignore setting
Each flagged job shown as a compact row
Shows: job title, company, reason flagged, time added
Clicking a row opens the detail panel
Reason for flagging shown prominently (e.g. "This job is asking if you're willing to relocate")
On Confirm → job moves to auto-apply queue using one of the 10 soft-reserved slots
If slots remain in today's quota, applies immediately
If quota full, rolls to next day's cycle
via the OpenAI API. Enrichment runs after each successful application, asynchronously (non-blocking — the worker loop continues to the next job without waiting).
Extract the salary or salary range from the following job description.
Return only a JSON object: { "min": number | null, "max": number | null, "currency": string | null }
If salary is not explicitly mentioned, return { "min": null, "max": null, "currency": null }.
Do not infer, estimate, or guess. Only extract if explicitly stated.
table — if company name exists and
is within 30 days, return cached summary
If not cached → call Tavily search API with query:
Pass top 3 Tavily results as context to GPT-4o mini
"In 2-3 sentences, summarize what this company does, where they are based, and what industry they operate in. Be factual and concise."
company summaries are shared across all users. If User A and User B both apply to the same company, the summary is only fetched once.
id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid()
id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid()
user_id UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES users(id) ON DELETE CASCADE
session_cookie_enc TEXT -- AES-256 encrypted cookie JSON
min_salary INT -- nullable, no filter if null
id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid()
user_id UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES users(id) ON DELETE CASCADE
linkedin_job_id TEXT NOT NULL -- used for duplicate detection
id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid()
id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid()
user_id UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES users(id) ON DELETE CASCADE
job_id UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES jobs(id) ON DELETE CASCADE
id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid()
user_id UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES users(id) ON DELETE CASCADE
List all job cards (supports filter params)
Confirm or skip a flagged job
Fetch or return cached company summary
│ │ │ ├── start.post.ts # sets stop_flag false, kicks off loop
│ │ ├── linkedin.service.ts # search, filter, Easy Apply steps, randomized delays
│ │ ├── screening.service.ts # detect relocation + custom screening questions
│ │ ├── ai.service.ts # salary parse + company summary logic
│ │ └── quota.service.ts # check, increment, reset daily quota
│ ├── encrypt.ts # AES-256 encrypt/decrypt for session cookie
│ └── auth.ts # session helpers for Nuxt Auth Utils
│ └── settings.vue # profile, targeting, LinkedIn auth, schedule
│ │ ├── JobCard.vue # expandable job card with all fields
│ │ ├── JobList.vue # catalogue grid with filter/search bar
│ │ └── ReviewDetail.vue # right panel — full detail + confirm/skip
Gmail integration — detect follow-up links from recruiters
